title: How Many Elementary Particles Are There, Really?
blurb: The Standard Model's classroom poster shows 17 elementary particles, but physicists asked to count nature's fundamental constituents give wildly different answers depending on how they treat color charges, antiparticles, and quantum field degrees of freedom. One Cambridge physicist told Quanta the true tally isn't even an integer, citing a 2011 calculation that complicates any clean census of the building blocks of matter.

title: Apple Vision Pro exec is reportedly leaving for OpenAI
blurb: Paul Meade, the Apple VP leading Vision Pro and the company's upcoming smart glasses, is reportedly leaving to join OpenAI's hardware team, according to Bloomberg's Mark Gurman. The move adds to OpenAI's hardware push alongside former Apple design chief Jony Ive, and Gurman ties the exit to a hardware-engineering shakeup under incoming CEO John Ternus that left some VPs feeling demoted.

title: Tokyo-based Sakana AI's Fugu and China-based 360's cybersecurity model Tulongfeng claim to rival Anthropic's banned Mythos and Fable 5 amid the US export ban (Kate Park/TechCrunch)
blurb: Sakana AI's Fugu and 360's Tulongfeng launched as Asian alternatives to Anthropic's Mythos and Fable 5, which are now blocked in China and parts of Asia under the US export ban. The Tokyo and Beijing models are positioned to capture customers cut off from US frontier labs, signaling that export controls are accelerating a parallel non-US model ecosystem.

title: Ukraine Says Russian Intelligence Used Fake Support Texts to Steal Messaging Credentials
blurb: Ukraine's Security Service and the FBI uncovered a long-running Russian intelligence campaign that sends fake messaging-platform support texts to trick officials, military personnel, and activists into handing over their account credentials. The targets span Ukraine, Europe, and the US, with related waves against Signal and WhatsApp tied to threat clusters including Star Blizzard and UNC5792, underscoring social engineering as the weak point even on encrypted apps.

title: Anonymous GitHub account mass-dropping undisclosed 0-days
blurb: An anonymous GitHub account is mass-publishing proof-of-concept exploits for undisclosed zero-day vulnerabilities affecting widely used software including FFmpeg, Firefox, libssh2, nghttp2, nmap, and PHP. The operator frames the drops as good-faith open-disclosure research consolidated into a single archive of 12 repos and 96 tracked entries, but releasing working exploits without coordinated vendor disclosure leaves affected projects and their users exposed.

title: Microsoft launches MAI-Code-1-Flash on GitHub Copilot
blurb: Microsoft made MAI-Code-1-Flash, its in-house coding model, generally available to GitHub Copilot Business and Enterprise subscribers, with admins enabling it through a Copilot settings policy. The model is tuned for low-latency code completion and agentic workflows in large codebases, billed at usage-based provider rates and deepening Microsoft's push to embed its own models into Copilot rather than relying solely on OpenAI and Anthropic.

title: ICYMI: Google adds Computer Use to Gemini 3.5 Flash
blurb: Google integrated computer use directly into Gemini 3.5 Flash, letting custom agents observe, reason, and act across browser, mobile, and desktop environments. Available now to developers and enterprises via the Gemini API and Enterprise Agent Platform, the feature moves agentic control out of the standalone Gemini 2.5 model and adds adversarial training plus optional safeguards against prompt injection.

title: Apple reportedly looks to blacklisted Chinese memory chips as RAM prices climb
blurb: Apple has reportedly asked the Trump administration for clearance to buy DRAM from ChangXin Memory Technologies, a top Chinese chipmaker on the Pentagon's list of Chinese military companies, as memory prices surge on AI data-center demand. The move is still a lobbying effort rather than a confirmed deal, but it signals how acute the supply crunch has become after Apple already raised MacBook and iPad prices over rising memory costs.

title: Your next EV battery could start life as a plastic water bottle
blurb: Penn State researchers converted discarded PET plastic, the material in water and soda bottles, into battery-grade synthetic graphite with a crystal structure more ordered than some commercial natural graphite. The process shreds PET, mixes in 2.5% graphene oxide as a template, and heats it without the metal catalysts that usually leave impurities, targeting a critical mineral whose demand is climbing with EVs and grid storage.

title: Cloudflare cut 1,100 jobs and then grew its engineering team by 45 percent, and its CEO says the pattern will repeat everywhere
blurb: Cloudflare's engineering headcount grew 45% to 1,894 in the weeks after it cut 1,100 jobs in May, according to BNP Paribas data from LinkedIn profiles reported by Business Insider. CEO Matthew Prince frames the shift around three roles: builders, sellers, and "measurers" like middle managers and analysts, arguing AI is eliminating the third group while companies keep hiring the first two.

title: Apple wants permission to buy memory from a blacklisted Chinese supplier
blurb: Apple is seeking an exception from the Trump administration to buy RAM from CXMT, a Chinese supplier blacklisted by the Pentagon over ties to the People's Liberation Army, according to the Financial Times. Surging memory and storage prices pushed Apple to raise prices on nearly all its products this week, but partnering with a Chinese military-linked firm carries reputational risk and has already drawn opposition from the House China committee chair.

title: SoftBank’s CEO isn’t the only one with questions about Elon Musk’s orbital data center hype
blurb: SoftBank CEO Masayoshi Son questioned Elon Musk's orbital data center plans at a shareholder meeting, arguing that building compute in space won't cut costs and will take too long to matter in the near-term AI race. Skeptics on TechCrunch's Equity podcast added that satellites needing replacement every few years mainly guarantee more launch business for SpaceX, which already rents out spare compute to players like Google and Anthropic.

title: Suspicious Discontinuities (2020)
blurb: US tax and subsidy cliffs create income thresholds where earning more leaves you worse off, sometimes incentivizing people to lose money.

title: Finding Android apps on the Google Play Store just got a lot easier thanks to Gemini
blurb: Google began rolling out a Google Play connected app for Gemini on Android, letting the assistant find apps by goal, open their Play Store listings, buy gift cards, and purchase in-app items inside the chat. The integration, first previewed at Google I/O 2026, joins recent Gemini tie-ins with Chrome, Wallet, Messages, and Phone, advancing Google's push to make Gemini the front end for tasks across Android.

title: Turn your site into a place people can bump into each other
blurb: Town Square is an open-source widget showing live stick-figure visitors browsing your site, with ephemeral chat and no accounts.

title: OpenAI’s poaching from Apple hints at ChatGPT-powered wearables coming for your face
blurb: Apple's longtime Vision Pro engineering leader Paul Meade is leaving to join OpenAI's hardware division, per Bloomberg. Meade joins former Apple veterans Jony Ive, Evans Hankey, and Tang Tan on a team assembled after OpenAI's $6.5B acquisition of Ive's startup io, with several new AI devices reportedly slated to launch over the next few years.

title: Apple’s looking at a politically radioactive fix for the memory crisis, and the US government isn’t happy about it
blurb: Apple is lobbying the White House for approval to buy DRAM from CXMT, a Chinese chipmaker on the Pentagon's military blacklist, to ease the memory shortage driving its recent price hikes. The request, made to the Commerce Department over a month ago, faces stiff political resistance, with House China Committee chair John Moolenaar calling the move a grave mistake.

title: Zuckerberg's war on whistleblowers
blurb: Cory Doctorow argues that Mark Zuckerberg's lawsuit demanding $111M and a permanent gag order against the author of a critical Meta memoir is a textbook Streisand effect, drawing attention to a book most people would otherwise ignore. The piece frames the legal campaign against whistleblowers as both an attempt at corporate censorship and a self-defeating move that amplifies the very criticism it aims to suppress.

title: AI executives and lobbyists say they are seeking regulatory clarity from the Trump administration but are wary of pressing for answers, fearing retaliation (Politico)
blurb: AI executives and lobbyists are seeking regulatory clarity from the Trump administration but hesitate to press for answers, fearing retaliation, according to Politico. The standoff leaves companies building under uncertain rules on issues like model oversight, export controls, and federal preemption of state AI laws, with little appetite to force decisions that could draw scrutiny.

title: ICYMI: Google adds Computer Use to Gemini 3.5 Flash
blurb: Google integrated computer use directly into Gemini 3.5 Flash, letting custom agents observe, reason, and act across browser, mobile, and desktop environments via the Gemini API and Enterprise Agent Platform. The capability, previously confined to a standalone Gemini 2.5 model, ships with adversarial training against prompt injection and optional safeguards that require user confirmation for sensitive actions or halt tasks when injections are detected.

title: OpenAI’s poaching from Apple hints at ChatGPT-powered wearables coming for your face
blurb: OpenAI hired Paul Meade, the Apple engineering leader behind the Vision Pro and its smart glasses effort, to join its hardware division. Meade joins former Apple veterans Jony Ive, Evans Hankey, and Tang Tan, who came together after OpenAI's $6.5B acquisition of Ive's startup io and are now developing several AI devices expected to launch over the next few years.

title: Microsoft launches MAI-Code-1-Flash on GitHub Copilot
blurb: Microsoft made MAI-Code-1-Flash, its in-house low-latency coding model, generally available to GitHub Copilot Business and Enterprise subscribers, provided administrators enable the policy in Copilot settings. The model is built for high-speed code completion and agentic workflows in large-scale development, and is billed at provider list rates under Copilot's usage-based pricing.

title: Apple Vision Pro exec is reportedly leaving for OpenAI
blurb: Paul Meade, the Apple vice president who led Vision Pro and the company's planned AI smart glasses, is reportedly leaving to join OpenAI's hardware team, according to Bloomberg's Mark Gurman. The move adds to OpenAI's device push alongside former Apple design chief Jony Ive, and Gurman ties the departure to a hardware-team shakeup under incoming CEO John Ternus.

title: Tokyo-based Sakana AI's Fugu and China-based 360's cybersecurity model Tulongfeng claim to rival Anthropic's banned Mythos and Fable 5 amid the US export ban (Kate Park/TechCrunch)
blurb: Sakana AI's Fugu and 360's Tulongfeng launched in Japan and China as claimed alternatives to Anthropic's Mythos and Fable 5, which are now blocked under the US export ban. The two models are positioned to fill demand in markets cut off from leading American systems, with 360 targeting cybersecurity use cases and Sakana pursuing general-purpose performance.

title: AI executives and lobbyists say they are seeking regulatory clarity from the Trump administration but are wary of pressing for answers, fearing retaliation (Politico)
blurb: AI executives and lobbyists are seeking regulatory clarity from the Trump administration but hesitate to press for answers, fearing retaliation. The dynamic leaves the industry navigating policy uncertainty on rules governing AI development and deployment while avoiding public conflict with an administration that has shown willingness to punish perceived critics.

title: Patronus AI grabs $50M in funding to stress-test AI agents in simulated environments
blurb: Patronus AI raised $50M in Series B funding led by Greenfield Partners, with participation from Lightspeed, Notable Capital, Datadog, and Samsung Ventures, bringing its total raised to $70M. The startup builds world models that let developers test autonomous agents in simulated environments mirroring real-world conditions, and says revenue grew 15-fold over the past year.

title: Cloudflare cut 1,100 jobs and then grew its engineering team by 45 percent, and its CEO says the pattern will repeat everywhere
blurb: Cloudflare's engineering headcount jumped 45% in the weeks after it cut 1,100 jobs in May, growing from 1,308 to 1,894 even as total staff shrank by a fifth, per BNP Paribas data drawn from LinkedIn. CEO Matthew Prince splits every company into builders, sellers, and measurers, and says AI is coming for the third group: the middle managers, ops, finance, and marketing coordinators whose job is mostly tracking and reporting. The signal for founders is that AI cuts land on oversight roles while the functions that build product and bring in revenue keep hiring.

title: Patronus AI grabs $50M in funding to stress-test AI agents in simulated environments
blurb: Patronus AI raised $50M in Series B funding led by Greenfield Partners to build world models that stress-test AI agents in simulated environments before they're trusted with real tasks. The pitch is that benchmark scores don't predict real-world reliability, so labs need digital sandboxes to run agents through messy scenarios first. Demand is steep: the startup says revenue grew 15-fold in the past year, with its environments used by virtually every major AI lab and dozens of startups. As agents move from answering questions to executing trades and booking appointments, the infrastructure for proving they work is becoming its own market.

title: Claude Code turned every engineer into three. Now companies need more product thinkers
blurb: Anthropic told its growth team to hire more product managers, not fewer, because Claude Code had quietly pushed its engineering org to ship at roughly three times its real headcount. The bottleneck moved off the IDE and onto the people deciding what to build. New monthly questions on Stack Overflow are down around 77% since ChatGPT launched, and for a growing share of developers the first command in a fresh terminal is now claude. The scarce skill is no longer typing the code, it is knowing which code is worth typing.

title: Tech Equity Sales Renew AI Debt-Binge Worries
blurb: Tech companies are raising equity at a pace not seen since the dot-com boom, and credit investors read it as a warning rather than a vote of confidence. The worry is that AI buildouts are increasingly funded by debt, so heavy stock sales suggest companies want a cushion before the bond market sours. If the firms with the best read on their own balance sheets are choosing to dilute now, the question for everyone holding their paper is what they see coming that the bondholders don't.

title: SoftBank’s CEO isn’t the only one with questions about Elon Musk’s orbital data center hype
blurb: SoftBank's Masayoshi Son told shareholders that orbital data centers won't meaningfully cut costs and will take too long to matter, arguing the AI race will be decided in the next few years rather than a decade out. The skepticism lands as nearly every compute-rich player, from Groq to a bankrupt shoemaker reborn as a cloud provider, pivots to renting out capacity, and SpaceX keeps signing compute leases while it builds toward an AI platform it claims could address a market the size of US GDP. The open question is how durable any of these neo-cloud businesses are once the compute crunch eases.

title: Anonymous GitHub account mass-dropping undisclosed 0-days
blurb: An anonymous GitHub account is mass-publishing PoC exploits for undisclosed 0-days across projects including FFmpeg, Firefox, PHP, and libssh2.

title: Turn your site into a place people can bump into each other
blurb: Town Square is an open-source widget that adds a live presence layer to any website, rendering current visitors as stick figures you can walk around, see what page they're reading, and message in real time. It runs without accounts, profiles, or persistent chat history, and ships with a public server so you can integrate it via the hosted service or self-host from the repo.

title: Microsoft launches MAI-Code-1-Flash on GitHub Copilot
blurb: Microsoft made MAI-Code-1-Flash, its in-house low-latency coding model, generally available to GitHub Copilot Business and Enterprise subscribers once admins enable the policy in Copilot settings. The model targets high-speed completion and agentic workflows for large teams, billed at usage-based provider list rates in line with Copilot's existing pricing.

title: Suspicious Discontinuities (2020)
blurb: Danluu argues that US tax and subsidy policy is riddled with "suspicious discontinuities," sharp income cutoffs where earning one more dollar can cost thousands, as with the ACA subsidy cliff that can spike an individual's health insurance by roughly $7,200/yr at $48,560. The takeaway for engineers who model incentives: hard thresholds create perverse behavior, and people will rationally optimize against them, even buying put options to lose money before year-end.

title: Wild Form designs with "no straight lines" for bar next to New York's Stonewall Inn
blurb: Wild Form Design Studio built a sculptural interior with "no straight lines" for Love Thy Neighbor, a queer bar that opened next to the Stonewall Inn in New York's West Village ahead of this weekend's Pride. The curved, line-free approach ties the space's form to its context, reading as a deliberate counterpoint to the rigid grids of conventional bar interiors.

title: Why your Claude Code needs a design stack
blurb: The author argues that Claude Code ships configured for engineering by default, and that designers should build a "design stack" — a one-time setup of brief, design knowledge, and tools handed to the agent upfront so it respects their workflow. The piece treats agent configuration as a craft decision rather than an engineering afterthought, making the case that the quality of output depends less on the model than on what context you give it.

title: AMLBot Puts Polymarket Phishing Toll at $3.1M Across 11 Wallets, Funds Traced to Ethereum
blurb: A supply-chain attack on Polymarket stole roughly $3.1M in PUSD across at least 11 wallets after a compromised third-party vendor injected malicious JavaScript that hijacked user transactions at the front-end layer, leaving the platform's Polygon smart contracts untouched. AMLBot traced the funds bridged from Polygon to Ethereum and swapped into about 1,893 ETH; Polymarket removed the malicious dependency, has not named the vendor, and committed to refunding affected users in full.

title: Post-Mythos Cybersecurity: Keep calm and carry on
blurb: The author argues that Claude Mythos, despite Anthropic's framing as a game-changer for automated zero-day discovery, represents gradual rather than discontinuous progress, with GPT-5.4 and Opus 4.6 not far behind on the same CTF and cyber-range benchmarks. He notes those benchmarks omit active defenders, defensive tooling, and alerting penalties, so they overstate impact against mature programs with a dedicated SOC. The takeaway for defenders is to keep doing the fundamentals rather than overhaul their programs in response to the hype.

title: Anonymous GitHub account mass-dropping undisclosed 0-days
blurb: An anonymous GitHub account operating as "bikini/exploitarium" has consolidated and published working proof-of-concept exploits for previously undisclosed bugs across widely deployed software including FFmpeg, Firefox, libssh2 (CVE-2026-55200), nghttp2, Nmap, PHP, and RustDesk. With functional PoCs now public ahead of coordinated disclosure, defenders should inventory exposure to the named projects, prioritize the libssh2 and RustDesk RCE/LPE chains, and monitor for opportunistic exploitation until vendor patches land.

title: Ukraine Says Russian Intelligence Used Fake Support Texts to Steal Messaging Credentials
blurb: Ukraine's SSU and the FBI disclosed a long-running Russian intelligence campaign that phishes messaging-app credentials by sending SMS posing as the platform's support bot, targeting government, military, and civil-society accounts across Ukraine, Europe, and the U.S. Related Signal and WhatsApp waves are tied to Star Blizzard, UNC5792, and UNC4221; defenders should enforce 2FA, audit active sessions, and never share recovery keys or confirmation codes.

title: Copilot in Excel: Built for the era of Frontier Finance
blurb: Microsoft introduced new Copilot capabilities in Excel for finance teams, adding skills for repeatable workflows like DCF builds and book closes, financial connectors for trusted data, and improved calculation traceability. Microsoft also partnered with the Financial Modeling Institute, whose library of real-world modeling cases now anchors how Copilot is benchmarked against the work FP&A, accounting, tax, and treasury teams do daily.

title: Apple reportedly looks to blacklisted Chinese memory chips as RAM prices climb
blurb: Apple has reportedly asked the Trump administration for clearance to buy DRAM from ChangXin Memory Technologies, a top Chinese maker already on the Pentagon's list of Chinese military companies, as soaring memory prices push it to seek alternatives to Samsung, SK Hynix, and Micron. The move is still a lobbying effort rather than a confirmed deal, but it signals how AI-driven memory demand is forcing hardware makers into politically sensitive supply choices.

title: Instagram is testing more ways to customize ‘Your Algorithm’
blurb: Instagram is testing new ways to access Your Algorithm, letting users tune topic preferences directly from the feed and Reels.

title: Claude Code turned every engineer into three. Now companies need more product thinkers
blurb: Anthropic told its growth team to hire more product managers after Claude Code pushed its engineering org to ship at roughly three times its headcount, moving the bottleneck from writing code to deciding what to build. For PMs, the shift raises the value of sharp prioritization and discovery, since the constraint on shipping is now the quality of product decisions rather than engineering throughput.

title: Average IBIT investor now down about 40% as spot bitcoin ETFs cap second-worst week on record
blurb: Spot bitcoin ETFs logged a $444.51 million net outflow on Friday, capping a seventh consecutive negative week and the longest weekly outflow streak since the products launched. The drawdown leaves the average IBIT holder down roughly 40%, marking the category's second-worst week on record as institutional demand continues to soften.

title: AMLBot Puts Polymarket Phishing Toll at $3.1M Across 11 Wallets, Funds Traced to Ethereum
blurb: Blockchain intelligence firm AMLBot pegged losses from Thursday's Polymarket supply-chain attack at roughly $3.1M in PUSD, the first forensically confirmed figure across 11-plus victim wallets, with funds bridged from Polygon to Ethereum and swapped into about 1,893 ETH. The attacker injected malicious JavaScript via a compromised vendor at Polymarket's front end, leaving its Polygon contracts untouched, and the platform has committed to refunding affected users in full.

title: Tech Equity Sales Renew AI Debt-Binge Worries
blurb: Tech companies are issuing stock at a pace not seen since the dot-com boom, a surge some investors read as a warning sign for bondholders exposed to AI-driven debt. The selling raises concern that the capital flooding into AI buildouts is outrunning fundamentals, leaving credit markets holding the risk if returns disappoint.

title: Copilot in Excel: Built for the era of Frontier Finance
blurb: Microsoft 365 Copilot in Excel added finance-specific features including skills for repeatable workflows like building a DCF or closing the books, new financial data connectors, and improved calculation traceability. The tools were evaluated against Financial Modeling Institute case libraries and pressure-tested inside Microsoft's own FP&A, accounting, tax, compliance, and treasury teams before shipping to external finance users.

title: Average IBIT investor now down about 40% as spot bitcoin ETFs cap second-worst week on record
blurb: Spot bitcoin ETFs logged a seventh straight week of net outflows, the longest negative run on record, capped by Friday's $444.51 million exit and leaving the average BlackRock IBIT holder down roughly 40%. The sustained redemptions signal institutional allocators are paring back exposure rather than buying the dip, undercutting the ETF category's role as a steady bid for bitcoin.
